part_title: Section Twelve: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components
part_short_title: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components
part_subtitle: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Successful integrated management of Tuta absoluta is not achieved by combining the greatest possible number of control methods in one program. It depends on selecting compatible methods that act in an integrated manner against different pest stages.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Successful integrated management of Tuta absoluta is not achieved by combining the greatest possible number of control methods in one program. It depends on selecting compatible methods that act in an integrated manner against different pest stages.

items.text: A program may fail despite the use of individually effective tools when conflicts occur among them, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A program may fail despite the use of individually effective tools when conflicts occur among them, such as:

items: Applying a highly toxic insecticide after releasing egg parasitoids.
Using a fungicide at the same time as an entomopathogenic fungus.
Mixing nematodes in a highly saline solution or one containing disinfectants.
Using two predators that engage in intraguild predation.
Applying a long-residual insecticide before releasing a sensitive natural enemy.
Combining two agents targeting the same stage when one kills the other.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Applying a highly toxic insecticide after releasing [[term:biological-control]]egg parasitoids[[/term]].
[[prepared]]Using a fungicide at the same time as an entomopathogenic fungus.
[[prepared]]Mixing nematodes in a highly saline solution or one containing disinfectants.
[[prepared]]Using two predators that engage in intraguild predation.
[[prepared]]Applying a long-residual insecticide before releasing a sensitive natural enemy.
[[prepared]]Combining two agents targeting the same stage when one kills the other.

items.text: Conversely, proper integration may:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conversely, proper integration may:

items: Target several stages of the pest life cycle.
Reduce the number of chemical sprays.
Increase program stability throughout the season.
Reduce the likelihood of pest resurgence after treatment.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Target several stages of the pest life cycle.
[[prepared]]Reduce the likelihood of pest resurgence after treatment.

items.text: Compatibility testing is therefore part of program design, not a secondary step reviewed only after failure occurs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compatibility testing is therefore part of program design, not a secondary step reviewed only after failure occurs.

items.text: Scientific Definition of Compatibility
Compatibility is the ability of two or more control methods to operate within the same system without causing an unacceptable negative effect on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compatibility is the ability of two or more control methods to operate within the same system without causing an unacceptable negative effect on:

items.text: Compatibility does not necessarily mean complete absence of any negative effect.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compatibility does not necessarily mean complete absence of any negative effect.

items.text: One method may cause a limited reduction in natural-enemy survival while the overall program remains economically and environmentally beneficial.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One method may cause a limited reduction in natural-enemy survival while the overall program remains economically and environmentally beneficial.

items.text: The final outcome should be evaluated at the level of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The final outcome should be evaluated at the level of:

items.text: Pest + natural enemy + plant + yield
And not on the basis of one factor alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And not on the basis of one factor alone.

items.text: Types of Compatibility
Toxicological Compatibility
This concerns whether an insecticide or product causes an unacceptable level of mortality in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This concerns whether an insecticide or product causes an unacceptable level of mortality in:

items.text: Behavioural Compatibility
This means not disrupting:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means not disrupting:

items.text: A natural enemy may survive exposure but become less able to detect the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A natural enemy may survive exposure but become less able to detect the pest.

items.text: Physiological Compatibility
This concerns avoiding unacceptable effects on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This concerns avoiding unacceptable effects on:

items.text: Physical and Chemical Compatibility
This concerns whether products can be mixed without:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This concerns whether products can be mixed without:

items: Killing a living organism in the tank.
Reduced stability of the active material.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Killing a living organism in the tank.

items.text: Temporal Compatibility
Two methods may be incompatible when used simultaneously but can be used within the same season if separated in time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Two methods may be incompatible when used simultaneously but can be used within the same season if separated in time.

items.text: Spatial Compatibility
Conflict can be reduced through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conflict can be reduced through:

items: Applying an insecticide to a defined hotspot.
Leaving untreated areas for natural enemies.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Applying an insecticide to a defined hotspot.

items.text: Outcomes of Interaction Between Two Methods
Combining two methods may produce four principal outcomes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Combining two methods may produce four principal outcomes.

items.text: Additive Effect
Additive Effect
The combined effect is close to the expected sum of the effects of each method used alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combined effect is close to the expected sum of the effects of each method used alone.

items.text: Example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Example:

items: A microbial agent kills part of the larval population.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A microbial agent kills part of the larval population.

items.text: Synergistic Effect
Synergistic Effect
The combined effect is greater than expected from the sum of the separate effects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combined effect is greater than expected from the sum of the separate effects.

items.text: This may occur when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may occur when:

items: A microbial agent weakens host immunity.
Parasitoid attack facilitates access by another agent.
The two methods efficiently target successive stages.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Parasitoid attack facilitates access by another agent.
[[prepared]]The two methods efficiently target successive stages.

items.text: Antagonistic Effect
Antagonistic Effect
The combined effect is lower than expected because one method interferes with the other.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combined effect is lower than expected because one method interferes with the other.

items.text: Examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Examples include:

items: The fungus kills the host before parasitoid development is completed.
A fungicide prevents germination of the entomopathogenic fungus.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The fungus kills the host before parasitoid development is completed.
[[prepared]]A fungicide prevents germination of the entomopathogenic fungus.

items.text: Neutral Effect
The combination does not measurably alter the effect of either method compared with separate use.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combination does not measurably alter the effect of either method compared with separate use.

items.text: Synergy should not be assumed merely because both methods reduce pest density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Synergy should not be assumed merely because both methods reduce pest density.

items.text: Why Does Incompatibility Occur?
Conflict occurs through one or more of the following pathways:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conflict occurs through one or more of the following pathways:

items: 1. Direct toxicity to the natural enemy.
3. Consumption of contaminated prey or host.
4. Reduced host quality after infection by another agent.
5. Killing the host before parasitoid development is completed.
9. Inhibition of fungal germination or nematode viability.
10. Alteration of searching or mating behaviour.
12. Differences in the timing of action of each method.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Direct toxicity to the natural enemy.
[[prepared]]3. Consumption of contaminated prey or host.
[[prepared]]4. Reduced host quality after infection by another agent.
[[prepared]]5. Killing the host before parasitoid development is completed.
[[prepared]]9. Inhibition of fungal germination or nematode viability.
[[prepared]]10. Alteration of searching or mating behaviour.
[[prepared]]12. Differences in the timing of action of each method.

items.text: Levels of Compatibility Assessment
A single laboratory result is insufficient for a final judgment on compatibility between a product and a natural enemy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A single laboratory result is insufficient for a final judgment on compatibility between a product and a natural enemy.

items.text: Effects should be studied at several levels.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Effects should be studied at several levels.

items.text: Direct Laboratory Test
The natural enemy is directly exposed to the insecticide or treated surface.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The natural enemy is directly exposed to the insecticide or treated surface.

items.text: This often represents the worst-case exposure scenario.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This often represents the worst-case exposure scenario.

items.text: Residual Test
The natural enemy is exposed to a leaf or surface treated with the insecticide after a defined time interval.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The natural enemy is exposed to a leaf or surface treated with the insecticide after a defined time interval.

items.text: This helps estimate the duration of residual hazard.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This helps estimate the duration of residual hazard.

items.text: Contaminated Host or Prey Test
The parasitoid or predator is exposed to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The parasitoid or predator is exposed to:

items: Prey that has ingested the insecticide.

items.text: Semi-Field Test
This is conducted on a whole plant, inside a cage, or in a small greenhouse.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is conducted on a whole plant, inside a cage, or in a small greenhouse.

items.text: Field Test
This measures:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This measures:

items.text: A laboratory test may show high toxicity while field risk is lower because exposure is reduced. The opposite may occur when residues persist for a long period.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A laboratory test may show high toxicity while field risk is lower because exposure is reduced. The opposite may occur when residues persist for a long period.

items.text: IOBC Classification of Pesticide Effects
The International Organisation for Biological and Integrated Control, IOBC, developed standardized methods for assessing pesticide effects on beneficial organisms.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The International Organisation for Biological and Integrated Control, IOBC, developed standardized methods for assessing pesticide effects on beneficial organisms.

items.text: The following categories are commonly used in laboratory tests:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following categories are commonly used in laboratory tests:

headers: Corrected Effect on the Natural Enemy

items.text: “Effect” may include more than direct mortality, such as reduced reproduction or activity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]“Effect” may include more than direct mortality, such as reduced reproduction or activity.

items.text: The classification from a laboratory test should not be transferred directly to the field because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The classification from a laboratory test should not be transferred directly to the field because:

items: Residual effects may persist for different periods.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Residual effects may persist for different periods.

items.text: Test details should be read, not only the class number.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Test details should be read, not only the class number.

items.text: Acute Toxicity and Sublethal Effects
Acute Toxicity
This causes mortality of the natural enemy within a short period after exposure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This causes mortality of the natural enemy within a short period after exposure.

items.text: Sublethal Effects
These do not kill the individual directly but may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These do not kill the individual directly but may cause:

items.text: An insecticide may appear compatible when mortality alone is measured while having severe population-level effects through reduced reproduction and searching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An insecticide may appear compatible when mortality alone is measured while having severe population-level effects through reduced reproduction and searching.

items.text: Differences in Susceptibility Among Species and Life Stages
No active ingredient is equally safe or harmful to all natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]] is equally safe or harmful to all natural enemies.

items.text: An insecticide may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An insecticide may be:

items: Low in toxicity to a predator.
Highly toxic to an egg parasitoid.
Less harmful to a parasitoid developing inside a host egg.
Highly harmful to a newly emerged adult.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Less harmful to a parasitoid developing inside a host egg.
[[prepared]]Highly harmful to a newly emerged adult.

items.text: Susceptibility also differs among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Susceptibility also differs among:

items: Different strains of the same species.

items.text: Results from one species should not be transferred to another without evidence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results from one species should not be transferred to another without evidence.

items.text: Routes of Parasitoid Exposure to Insecticides
Parasitoids may be exposed through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Parasitoids may be exposed through:

items: 3. Contact with treated eggs or larvae.
6. Developing inside a host exposed to the insecticide.
7. Emerging through a surface bearing residues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]3. Contact with treated eggs or larvae.
[[prepared]]6. Developing inside a host exposed to the insecticide.
[[prepared]]7. Emerging through a surface bearing residues.

items.text: The outcome varies according to parasitoid stage and its location relative to the host body.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The outcome varies according to parasitoid stage and its location relative to the host body.

items.text: Compatibility of Insecticides with Trichogramma
Minute adult Trichogramma parasitoids are highly exposed to insecticide residues on leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Minute adult Trichogramma parasitoids are highly exposed to insecticide residues on leaves.

items.text: Insecticides may affect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insecticides may affect:

items.text: The embryo and larva developing within the host egg receive partial protection from direct spray, but this protection is not complete because the insecticide may:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The embryo and larva developing within the host egg receive partial protection from direct spray, but this protection is not complete because the insecticide may:

items: Affect the host and its resources.
Remain as a residue around the exit hole.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Remain as a residue around the exit hole.

items.text: Timing of Insecticide Application Relative to Trichogramma Cards
When cards containing parasitized eggs are used:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When cards containing parasitized eggs are used:

items: They should not be installed before applying an incompatible insecticide.
The duration of toxic residues should be known.
Release should be delayed until risk declines to an acceptable level.
Cards should be temporarily removed only when allowed by the product program and when this does not interfere with emergence timing.
Cards should not be sprayed directly.
They should not be placed on leaves still wet with insecticide.
Spray time and expected parasitoid-emergence time should be recorded.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]They should not be installed before applying an incompatible insecticide.
[[prepared]]The duration of toxic residues should be known.
[[prepared]]Release should be delayed until risk declines to an acceptable level.
[[prepared]]Cards should be temporarily removed only when allowed by the product program and when this does not interfere with emergence timing.
[[prepared]]They should not be placed on leaves still wet with insecticide.
[[prepared]]Spray time and expected parasitoid-emergence time should be recorded.

items.text: No single time interval is suitable for all insecticides. The hazard from some active ingredients may persist for hours, while that from others may last for days or weeks.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]No single time interval is suitable for all insecticides. The hazard from some active ingredients may persist for hours, while that from others may last for days or weeks.

items.text: Insecticides and Trichogramma: Variation in Results
Studies show that some neuroactive insecticides can cause high mortality or substantial sublethal effects on egg parasitoids.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies show that some neuroactive insecticides can cause high mortality or substantial sublethal effects on egg parasitoids.

items.text: Studies on Trichogramma species used against Tuta absoluta have reported that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies on Trichogramma species used against Tuta absoluta have reported that:

items: Spinosad may cause high acute toxicity to adults in some test systems.
Mixtures containing Emamectin benzoate and Lufenuron may affect reproduction and some life-history traits.
Diamide effects vary according to the active ingredient, species, and exposure stage.
Bt is often more compatible when used correctly because it targets larvae that ingest it.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Spinosad may cause high acute toxicity to adults in some test systems.
[[prepared]]Mixtures containing Emamectin benzoate and Lufenuron may affect reproduction and some life-history traits.
[[prepared]]Diamide effects vary according to the active ingredient, species, and exposure stage.
[[prepared]]Bt is often more compatible when used correctly because it targets larvae that ingest it.

items.text: These findings should not be converted into a permanent classification for every product because formulation, concentration, life stage, and test method change the outcome.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These findings should not be converted into a permanent classification for every product because formulation, concentration, life stage, and test method change the outcome.

items.text: Compatibility of Insecticides with Larval Parasitoids
Larval parasitoids face two hazards:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval parasitoids face two hazards:

items: 1. Direct insecticide effects on the parasitoid.
2. Indirect effects through host mortality or reduced host quality.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Direct insecticide effects on the parasitoid.
[[prepared]]2. Indirect effects through host mortality or reduced host quality.

items.text: The effect differs according to the parasitoid’s biology.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The effect differs according to the parasitoid’s biology.

items.text: Endoparasitoid
It may be partly protected inside the host body but is affected when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may be partly protected inside the host body but is affected when:

items: The insecticide penetrates the host body.

items.text: Ectoparasitoid
It is more directly exposed to residues on the host or mine surface.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is more directly exposed to residues on the host or mine surface.

items.text: Host-Feeding Parasitoid
It may ingest insecticide residues while feeding on larval fluids.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may ingest insecticide residues while feeding on larval fluids.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Evaluation should therefore include:

items.text: Necremnus tutae and Microbial Agents
Recent experiments have shown that combining N. tutae with some biological agents may produce an additive reduction in Tuta absoluta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recent experiments have shown that combining N. tutae with some biological agents may produce an additive reduction in Tuta absoluta.

items.text: In one test:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one test:

items.text: Were among the agents showing relatively high compatibility with N. tutae and the other parasitoid used in the study.

items.text: Compatibility of Insecticides with Mirid Predators
Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis may be exposed through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis may be exposed through:

items: Drinking water droplets or plant exudates.

items.text: Insecticides may affect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insecticides may affect:

items.text: One comparative study reported that Indoxacarb caused higher mortality than Azadirachtin and Spinosad in the two tested predators, with effects differing between M. pygmaeus nymphs and N. tenuis females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One comparative study reported that Indoxacarb caused higher mortality than Azadirachtin and Spinosad in the two tested predators, with effects differing between M. pygmaeus nymphs and N. tenuis females.

items.text: This result shows that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This result shows that:

items: The two species do not have equal susceptibility.
Nymphal susceptibility differs from adult susceptibility.
A material selective to one natural enemy may be less selective to another.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The two species do not have equal susceptibility.

items.text: Insecticides and Predator Function
Predator survival after treatment is not sufficient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predator survival after treatment is not sufficient.

items.text: Sublethal exposure may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sublethal exposure may cause:

items: Reduced number of eggs or larvae consumed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reduced number of eggs or larvae consumed.

items.text: Plant damage by N. tenuis may increase if prey density declines suddenly after insecticide use while the predator remains alive in the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Plant damage by N. tenuis may increase if prey density declines suddenly after insecticide use while the predator remains alive in the crop.

items.text: The predator-to-prey ratio should therefore be evaluated after treatment, not predator survival alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The predator-to-prey ratio should therefore be evaluated after treatment, not predator survival alone.

items.text: Predators and Broad-Spectrum Insecticides
Broad-spectrum insecticides, including many:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Broad-spectrum insecticides, including many:

items.text: Are more likely to disrupt predator and parasitoid communities.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Are more likely to disrupt predator and parasitoid communities.

items.text: Killing natural enemies may result in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Killing natural enemies may result in:

items: A shift toward complete chemical dependence.

items.text: This does not mean that every active ingredient within a broad-spectrum group has identical toxicity, but the burden of proving compatibility is greater.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean that every active ingredient within a broad-spectrum group has identical toxicity, but the burden of proving compatibility is greater.

items.text: Functional Compatibility of Bacillus thuringiensis
Bt targets lepidopteran larvae after ingestion of active proteins and therefore generally has less direct effect on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Bt targets lepidopteran larvae after ingestion of active proteins and therefore generally has less direct effect on:

items: Insects lacking the appropriate toxin receptors.

items.text: It can be integrated with egg parasitoids because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It can be integrated with egg parasitoids because:

items: The parasitoid prevents hatching of part of the egg population.
Bt targets larvae emerging from non-parasitized eggs.

[[prepared]]Bt targets larvae emerging from non-parasitized eggs.

items.text: It can also be integrated with predators consuming:

items: Larvae that did not ingest a lethal dose.

items.text: Entomopathogenic Fungi and Parasitoids
Combining an entomopathogenic fungus with a parasitoid may produce one of the following outcomes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Combining an entomopathogenic fungus with a parasitoid may produce one of the following outcomes.

items.text: Complementarity
The fungus infects larvae not reached by the parasitoid, while the parasitoid attacks other individuals.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fungus infects larvae not reached by the parasitoid, while the parasitoid attacks other individuals.

items.text: Additive Effect
Each agent contributes part of the reduction without strongly interfering with the other.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Each agent contributes part of the reduction without strongly interfering with the other.

items.text: Conflict
The fungus may infect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fungus may infect:

items: The immature stage developing on the host.
The host before parasitoid development is completed.

[[prepared]]The host before parasitoid development is completed.

items.text: The interaction depends on:

items: Whether the parasitoid develops inside or outside the host.

items.text: It is insufficient to describe Beauveria bassiana as generally compatible because strains and formulations differ.

items.text: Temporal Separation Between Fungi and Parasitoids
Conflict can be reduced by sequencing the applications.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Conflict can be reduced by sequencing the applications.

items.text: Possible systems include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Possible systems include:

items: Releasing the parasitoid first, then applying the fungus after a sensitive parasitoid stage has passed.
Applying the fungus first, then delaying adult-parasitoid release until active spores on the surface have declined.
Restricting the fungus to hotspots and using parasitoids in the rest of the field.
Applying the fungus to soil while parasitoids are used on the foliage.

Copper and sulfur compounds may have antifungal effects to varying degrees.

items.text: Insect Viruses and Parasitoids
Granuloviruses infect larvae after ingestion of occlusion bodies, whereas larval parasitoids may attack different instars.

Ability of some parasitoids to attack older instars.

items.text: However, infection timing is important. If the virus kills the host before parasitoid development is completed, parasitoid emergence may decline.

Natural origin does not mean that a material is compatible with natural enemies.

Sex pheromones generally do not act as lethal toxins and are therefore among the tools most readily integrated with natural enemies.

Effects of mating disruption on male distribution rather than females alone.
Avoiding placement of release cards inside traps.
Avoiding sticky surfaces near beneficial-insect release points.
Potential capture of non-target organisms in non-selective traps.

Egg parasitoids and predators may complement one another when:

The predator consumes healthy eggs and hatching larvae.
Alternative prey are available to the predator.
The predator can distinguish parasitized eggs or consumes fewer of them.

items.text: Ecological Selectivity and Physiological Selectivity
Physiological Selectivity
The active ingredient is more toxic to the pest than to the natural enemy because of differences in receptors, metabolism, or exposure.

This is achieved through application method, such as:

items.text: An insecticide may lack physiological selectivity but its damage can be reduced through appropriate ecological selectivity.

items.text: Steps for Evaluating Any New Combination
Before introducing two materials or agents together, the following sequence should be followed:

2. Identify the target stage of each method.
6. Review physical compatibility in the spray tank.
7. Assess susceptibility of the strain or natural enemy used.
9. Measure both the pest and the natural enemy.
12. Discontinue the combination if its net effect is lower than that of using the two methods separately.

items.text: Compatibility Program in Open Fields
The basic steps include:

items: Reviewing the effect of each active ingredient on the dominant species.
Avoiding unnecessary spraying during peak parasitoid activity.
Protecting supportive plant strips from spray drift.
Separating application of entomopathogenic fungi from incompatible fungicides.
Avoiding addition of fertilizers or acids to nematodes without testing.
Modifying the program when a severe population decline occurs.

Greenhouses allow greater control but require precise coordination because of intensive release programs.

Treating hotspots rather than the entire greenhouse where possible.
Providing supplementary food after materials that reduce prey.
Monitoring plant damage caused by N. tenuis.
Avoiding long-residual insecticides before a new release.
Washing or replacing some surfaces or plant parts when hazardous residues persist.

Active ingredients used against whiteflies or thrips may severely affect natural enemies used against Tuta absoluta.

Protecting natural enemies contributes to insecticide-resistance management by:

Reducing the size of the population subjected to selection.
Extending the useful life of chemical groups.

A combination of methods may be biologically compatible but economically impractical.

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Assuming Every Biopesticide Is Safe to Natural Enemies
Fungi, oils, or extracts may affect parasitoids and predators.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fungi, oils, or extracts may affect parasitoids and predators.

items.text: Error Two: Judging Compatibility from Mortality Alone
Strong behavioural and reproductive effects may occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Strong behavioural and reproductive effects may occur.

items.text: Error Three: Transferring Results from One Species to Another
Natural-enemy susceptibility differs among species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural-enemy susceptibility differs among species.

items.text: Error Four: Transferring Results from an Active Ingredient to All Commercial Formulations
Adjuvants may alter toxicity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adjuvants may alter toxicity.

items.text: Error Five: Treating a Successful Jar Test as Evidence of Biological Compatibility
It demonstrates physical compatibility only.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It demonstrates physical compatibility only.

items.text: Error Six: Mixing an Entomopathogenic Fungus with a Fungicide
Spores may die before application.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Spores may die before application.

items.text: Error Seven: Mixing Nematodes with Fertilizers or Disinfectants Without Testing
They may lose movement and infectivity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may lose movement and infectivity.

items.text: Error Eight: Releasing Trichogramma on Leaves Carrying Hazardous Residues
Adults may die immediately after emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adults may die immediately after emergence.

items.text: Error Nine: Removing Parasitized Leaves
This causes loss of parasitoids.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This causes loss of parasitoids.

items.text: Error Ten: Using an Insecticide Against Another Pest Without Considering Its Effect on the Tuta Program
It may disrupt biological control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may disrupt biological control.

items.text: Error Eleven: Combining Several Predators Without Study
Competition or intraguild predation may occur.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Competition or intraguild predation may occur.

items.text: Error Twelve: Using One Fixed Time Interval for All Insecticides
Residual persistence differs among active ingredients.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residual persistence differs among active ingredients.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Relying on the Term “Selective” in Marketing Claims
The natural-enemy species, life stage, and test method should be known.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The natural-enemy species, life stage, and test method should be known.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Failing to Monitor Natural Enemies After Treatment
Program effects cannot be determined without measurement.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Program effects cannot be determined without measurement.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Treating Compatibility as a Permanent Property
It may change with dose, temperature, formulation, and application method.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may change with dose, temperature, formulation, and application method.

items.text: Practical Priorities in Egypt
Scientific priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Scientific priorities are:

items: 1. Establish a local database on the effects of registered insecticides on natural enemies.
2. Test T. achaeae, T. euproctidis, and local strains.
3. Evaluate N. tenuis and M. pygmaeus under Egyptian temperatures.
4. Do not transfer classifications from products used in other countries without matching formulation and dose.
5. Establish time-interval tables before natural-enemy release.
6. Include disease-control products in compatibility assessment.
7. Test copper and sulfur with the fungal strains used.
8. Evaluate nematode viability in local irrigation and spray water.
9. Analyze the effects of water salinity, bicarbonates, and disinfectants.
10. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies in open fields.
12. Prevent direct spray on parasitoid cards and release points.
13. Train workers to recognize parasitized eggs.
14. Include nursery applications in the field record.
15. Measure sublethal effects, not mortality alone.
16. Link compatibility to reduced fruit infestation and economic return.
17. Do not describe any product as “safe” without specifying the natural enemy and life stage.
18. Update compatibility tables when registrations and commercial formulations change.

[[prepared]]2. Test T. achaeae, T. euproctidis, and local strains.
[[prepared]]3. Evaluate N. tenuis and M. pygmaeus under Egyptian temperatures.
[[prepared]]4. Do not transfer classifications from products used in other countries without matching formulation and dose.
[[prepared]]5. Establish time-interval tables before natural-enemy release.
[[prepared]]6. Include disease-control products in compatibility assessment.
[[prepared]]7. Test copper and sulfur with the fungal strains used.
[[prepared]]8. Evaluate nematode viability in local irrigation and spray water.
[[prepared]]9. Analyze the effects of water salinity, bicarbonates, and disinfectants.
[[prepared]]10. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies in open fields.
[[prepared]]12. Prevent direct spray on parasitoid cards and release points.
[[prepared]]13. Train workers to recognize parasitized eggs.
[[prepared]]14. Include nursery applications in the field record.
[[prepared]]15. Measure sublethal effects, not mortality alone.
[[prepared]]16. Link compatibility to reduced fruit infestation and economic return.
[[prepared]]17. Do not describe any product as “safe” without specifying the natural enemy and life stage.
[[prepared]]18. Update compatibility tables when registrations and commercial formulations change.

items.text: Scientific Summary of Section Twelve
Compatibility among control methods is not an absolute binary property. It is an outcome that changes according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compatibility among control methods is not an absolute binary property. It is an outcome that changes according to:

items.text: Acute toxicity, sublethal effects, and residues should all be assessed rather than direct mortality alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Acute toxicity, sublethal effects, and residues should all be assessed rather than direct mortality alone.

items.text: IOBC methods provide a standardized framework for assessing effects on natural enemies, but laboratory results should not be transferred directly to field conditions without semi-field and field tests.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]IOBC methods provide a standardized framework for assessing effects on natural enemies, but laboratory results should not be transferred directly to field conditions without semi-field and field tests.

items.text: Egg parasitoids, particularly Trichogramma, are sensitive to direct exposure and residues. Insecticide applications should therefore be coordinated with card placement and adult emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg parasitoids, particularly Trichogramma, are sensitive to direct exposure and residues. Insecticide applications should therefore be coordinated with card placement and adult emergence.

items.text: The susceptibility of Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis to the same active ingredient also differs, and a product cannot be considered safe to mirids as one undifferentiated group.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The susceptibility of Macrolophus pygmaeus and Nesidiocoris tenuis to the same active ingredient also differs, and a product cannot be considered safe to mirids as one undifferentiated group.

items.text: Bt is among the agents most readily integrated with egg parasitoids and predators in many programs because it targets larvae after ingestion, while the specific product and adjuvants still require assessment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Bt is among the agents most readily integrated with egg parasitoids and predators in many programs because it targets larvae after ingestion, while the specific product and adjuvants still require assessment.

items.text: Recent studies have shown relatively good compatibility between B. bassiana, Bt, and some parasitoids of Tuta larvae, whereas other materials have shown clear toxicity or behavioural and reproductive effects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recent studies have shown relatively good compatibility between B. bassiana, Bt, and some parasitoids of Tuta larvae, whereas other materials have shown clear toxicity or behavioural and reproductive effects.

items.text: Granuloviruses show relatively high compatibility with some larval parasitoids because they target different larval ages, but the sequence of infection and parasitism remains important.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Granuloviruses show relatively high compatibility with some larval parasitoids because they target different larval ages, but the sequence of infection and parasitism remains important.

items.text: Entomopathogenic fungi should be carefully coordinated with disease-control fungicides because fungicide residues may prevent spore germination even without direct tank mixing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Entomopathogenic fungi should be carefully coordinated with disease-control fungicides because fungicide residues may prevent spore germination even without direct tank mixing.

items.text: Nematodes should not be mixed with an insecticide, fertilizer, or disinfectant unless their movement and infectivity remain verified.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Nematodes should not be mixed with an insecticide, fertilizer, or disinfectant unless their movement and infectivity remain verified.

items.text: Every IPM program should include a compatibility matrix and a chronological record specifying:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every IPM program should include a compatibility matrix and a chronological record specifying:

items.text: An integrated program succeeds not through the number of its components but through the ability of those components to function together without one undermining another.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An integrated program succeeds not through the number of its components but through the ability of those components to function together without one undermining another.

items.text: References

meta.part_title: Section Twelve: Compatibility Among Integrated Pest Management Components
